title: Interest rates and Bank Rate: our latest decision
summary: The Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee held Bank Rate at 3.75% on 19 March 2026, citing that the war in the Middle East has disrupted energy supply and will push inflation higher than previously expected. The current inflation rate stands at 3%, above the 2% target.
key_points: Bank Rate maintained at 3.75% — unchanged from the prior meeting
This follows six rate cuts since August 2024
War in the Middle East has disrupted transportation and supply of energy, raising energy prices
Inflation will be higher than expected this year; current inflation is 3% versus the 2% target
Higher energy costs could slow economic growth as households and businesses have less disposable income
MPC is monitoring the situation closely and will do what is necessary to return inflation to the 2% target sustainably
Next MPC meeting is scheduled for 30 April 2026
why_it_matters: The Bank of England has paused its easing cycle and signaled that external supply shocks from geopolitical tensions are complicating the inflation outlook. This suggests the committee is data-dependent and may hold rates higher for longer if energy-driven inflation persists, affecting borrowing costs across the UK economy.
macro_context: The Bank cites a supply-side inflation shock from Middle East conflict disrupting global energy markets. Unlike demand-driven inflation, monetary policy has limited tools to address energy price spikes, creating uncertainty around the medium-term rate path. The committee must assess whether higher energy costs translate into sustained second-round effects on broader price-setting behavior.
notes: Source is a high-trust domain (Bank of England official website) and directly relevant to macro catalysts. Published 12 days before TODAY, making it a recent decision. The page explicitly connects geopolitical risk (Middle East war) to energy price inflation and the BoE's policy path. No forward guidance on next rate action is given beyond monitoring the situation.
